summary:
Gojoseon (c. 400–108 BC in its documented phase) was the first kingdom of Korea according to historical tradition, whose legendary founding by the demigod Dangun in 2333 BC makes it central to Korean national identity, though the historical kingdom centered on the Taedong River basin is attested only from the early centuries BC before its conquest by the Han Chinese.

The legendary origin story of Dangun — born of a heavenly father and a bear-woman who had transformed into a human — remains the founding myth of Korean civilization, celebrated on National Foundation Day (October 3). The historical Gojoseon interacted with the Chinese states during the [[event:1111|Warring States Period]] and later with the [[event:77|Han Dynasty]]. Its later rulers, possibly of Chinese refugee stock (the Wiman Joseon phase), maintained considerable autonomy by playing Han diplomatic interests against their independence. Emperor Wu of Han launched a military campaign against it in [[event:1167|Fall of Gojoseon]] (109–108 BC), bringing the Korean peninsula into the Chinese cultural sphere. Four Han commanderies were established, but Korean political organization and cultural identity persisted, forming the basis for the Three Kingdoms period that followed.
